title: Pulmonary Infections in Patients with Human Immunodeficiency Virus Infection
journal: Seminars in Respiratory and Critical Care Medicine
one_line_summary:
- **Epidemiology** of pulmonary infections in **PWH**  
- **Risk factors** for pulmonary infections in **PWH**  
- **Diagnosis** of pulmonary infections in **PWH**  
- **Management** of pulmonary infections in **PWH**  
- **Prevention** of pulmonary infections in **PWH**  
- Highlighting **shifting trends** and the impact of **ART** on outcomes
key_pearls: CD4 T-cell counts do not predict acute **mortality** in CAP and must not be used to deny ICU-level care.

Strictly reserve adjunctive corticosteroids for PCP patients meeting blood gas criteria (PaO2 < **70 mmHg** or A-a gradient >= **35 mmHg**); avoid folinic acid co-administration due to increased **mortality**.
Implement routine HIV screening for all adult patients presenting to the emergency department with CAP, given the high rate of undiagnosed HIV in this population.
Utilize the urine LAM test as a rapid diagnostic adjunct for TB in **critically** ill patients with advanced HIV (CD4 < 100 cells/uL) or signs of disseminated disease.
population: People living with HIV (PWH), including those with advanced immunosuppression (CD4 < 200 cells/uL), and non-HIV immunocompromised patients.

sections.heading: Global Epidemiology & Shifting Trends
sections.content: Globally, approximately 40.8 million people live with HIV (PWH) as of 2024, with 1.3 million new infections and 630,000 AIDS-related deaths. About 77% of PWH have access to ART. The incidence of Community-Acquired Pneumonia (CAP) in PWH in high-income settings is **significantly** higher than in the general population (1,529 vs. 69 per 100,000 patient-years), with the highest risk in those not on ART with CD4 < 500 cells/uL (8,023 per 100,000 patient-years). Even PWH with CD4 > 500 cells/uL on ART still have a higher incidence. In sub-Saharan Africa, Tuberculosis (TB) is the leading cause of CAP, followed by pneumococcal pneumonia and PCP, with ICU **mortality** rates reaching 50%. There's a **notable** epidemiological shift for Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PCP), with increasing diagnoses in non-HIV immunocompromised patients (e.g., hematologic malignancies [32%], solid tumors [22%], autoimmune diseases [18%]). PWH are 16 times more likely to develop active TB, accounting for 161,000 deaths from HIV-associated TB in 2023.

sections.heading: Bacterial Community-Acquired Pneumonia (CAP)
sections.content: Risk factors for CAP in PWH include chronic comorbidities (COPD, cardiovascular disease, liver disease), smoking, hazardous alcohol use, low CD4 counts, detectable viral load, and prior pneumonia. Specifically, CD4 counts < 50 cells/uL carry a Hazard Ratio (HR) of 7.68 for CAP, while a viral load >= 200 copies/mL has an HR of 1.92, and a previous pneumonia episode an HR of 4.82. Clinical presentation is similar to non-HIV patients, though fever may be absent in severe immunosuppression. Sepsis/septic shock occurs in 10-20% of hospitalized PWH with CAP. Streptococcus pneumoniae is the leading pathogen (30-40% of solved cases), with outcomes in virologically suppressed PWH (CD4 > 350 cells/uL) comparable to non-HIV controls. Other pathogens include Staphylococcus aureus (19%), Haemophilus influenzae (14%), Klebsiella pneumoniae, and Pseudomonas aeruginosa (associated with severe disease). 5-10% of PWH with CAP require ICU admission, with ICU **mortality** ranging from 20-48%. CD4 counts do not predict acute outcomes and should not guide ICU admission. Vaccination guidelines recommend annual influenza (quadrivalent), pneumococcal (PCV15 followed by PPSV23 or PCV20/PCV21 alone), RSV for PWH >= 75 years (or 60-74 if high risk), and COVID-19 for all PWH >= 18 years.
section_pearls: Pneumococcal and influenza vaccination are crucial for PWH, and outcomes for virologically suppressed PWH with CAP are similar to the general population.
CD4 T-cell counts do not predict acute mortality in CAP and should not guide ICU admission decisions.

sections.heading: Pneumocystis jirovecii Pneumonia (PCP)
sections.content: PCP risk factors include CD4 count < 200 cells/uL, with most cases occurring in undiagnosed or non-adherent PWH. Clinical presentation is sub-acute (over ~3 weeks) with low-grade fever, progressive dyspnea, non-productive cough, and fatigue. **Mortality** averages 15%, rising to 58% if ICU admission is required. Radiographic findings include bilateral interstitial/perihilar alveolar infiltrates, with HRCT showing ground-glass opacities (GGO) with peripheral sparing (41%), mosaic patterns (29%), and cysts (21%). Microbiological diagnosis relies on microscopy (induced sputum or BAL with specific stains), immunofluorescence (IFA) on BAL (sensitivity 99%), and molecular tests (PCR/LAMP) for high sensitivity. Serum Beta-D-glucan (BG) has high sensitivity (92%) and a negative test reliably rules out PCP in PWH. First-line treatment for moderate-to-severe PCP is IV Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) for 21 days (TMP 15-**20 mg**/kg/day, SMX 75-**100 mg**/kg/day). Mild-to-moderate cases can use oral TMP-SMX. Alternative regimens include primaquine plus clindamycin or pentamidine. Adjunctive corticosteroids are indicated if PaO2 < **70 mmHg** or A-a gradient >= **35 mmHg**, with a specific prednisone tapering regimen. Folinic acid is **contraindicated** due to increased risk of treatment failure and death. Primary prophylaxis is initiated if CD4 < 200 cells/uL (or 100-200 if viral load detectable) and can be stopped if CD4 >= 200 cells/uL for >= 3 months on ART. Secondary prophylaxis follows an episode of PCP.
section_pearls: Serum Beta-D-glucan (BG) has high sensitivity (92%) and a negative test reliably rules out PCP in PWH.
Adjunctive corticosteroids for PCP are indicated only if PaO2 < 70 mmHg or A-a gradient >= 35 mmHg, and folinic acid is contraindicated.

sections.heading: Mycobacterium tuberculosis (TB)
sections.content:
- **TB clinical presentation correlates with immune status**  
  - **People with HIV (PWH) with CD4 > 200 cells/µL** present with:  
    - Typical **upper lobe infiltrates**  
    - **Cavitation**  
  - **PWH with CD4 < 200 cells/µL** present atypically with:  
    - **Lower/middle lobe infiltrates**  
    - Lack of **cavitation**  
    - **Mediastinal lymphadenopathy**  
    - High rates of **extrapulmonary** or **disseminated TB**  

- **Microbiological diagnosis** involves:  
  - **Sputum smear (AFB)** and **culture**  
  - **Nucleic Acid Amplification (NAA) tests** – **Xpert MTB/RIF** or **Xpert Ultra** for sensitivity and **rifampin resistance** detection  
  - **Urine lipoarabinomannan (LAM) antigen test** for advanced HIV (**sensitivity 30‑70%**)  

- **Treatment of drug‑susceptible TB**  
  1. **Intensive phase (8 weeks)** – **Isoniazid** + **Rifampin** (or **Rifabutin**) + **Pyrazinamide** + **Ethambutol** + **Pyridoxine**  
  2. **Continuation phase (18 weeks)** – **Rifampin** + **Isoniazid** + **Pyridoxine**  
  - Total duration: **6 months** (longer for severe or extrapulmonary disease)  

- **Alternative regimens** exist for **Efavirenz‑based ART**  

- **Drug‑resistant TB** (e.g., **Isoniazid‑resistant**) is treated with:  
  - A **fluoroquinolone** plus **rifampin**, **ethambutol**, and **pyrazinamide** for **6 months**  

- **Rifamycin‑resistant TB** is treated with the **BPaLM/BPaL regimen** (**Pretomanid**, **Linezolid**, **Moxifloxacin**, **Bedaquiline**) for **24 weeks**
section_pearls: Nucleic Acid Amplification (NAA) tests are highly sensitive for TB diagnosis and detect rifampin resistance.
The urine LAM antigen test is a useful rapid diagnostic adjunct for TB in critically ill patients with advanced HIV (CD4 < 100 cells/uL) or disseminated disease.

strengths_limitations:
- This review provides detailed diagnostic and therapeutic pathways for major pulmonary pathogens in **PWH**, highlighting epidemiological shifts and precise drug dosing.  
- Limitations:  
  - Lack of extensive discussion on viral co‑infections (e.g., **CMV**, **influenza**, **COVID-19**)  
  - Does not fully address the management of **Immune Reconstitution Inflammatory Syndrome (IRIS)** beyond recommending **corticosteroids**.
